professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As we've explored this semester, the debate over whether AI research breakthroughs should be released as open-source code or kept proprietary has intensified. Companies like Meta have released their large language models openly, while others have moved toward closed, restricted systems. Open-source supporters argue this accelerates collective progress and prevents corporate monopolies. Opponents warn about misuse and safety risks. Where do you stand on this? Should AI labs make their model weights publicly available, or is restricted release the more responsible path?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think open-source is the better path, honestly. When everyone can examine the code, researchers around the world can spot security flaws faster than any single company could. My friend works at a small startup and they literally cannot function without open models — they would be priced out by Big Tech otherwise. Open release also keeps the field accountable, because hiding capabilities makes it nearly impossible for outside experts to evaluate safety claims.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I see what Claire is saying, but I lean the other way. Releasing powerful models openly means handing capable tools to anyone, including bad actors who can fine-tune them for scams or disinformation. Last year there were already reports of open models being used to generate convincing fake voices for phone fraud. Some kind of vetting before release seems reasonable. I'm not saying close everything off — maybe staged release or licensed access strikes a better balance.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we're going to talk about taxes on sugary beverages. Several countries and U.S. cities have introduced taxes on sodas and sweetened drinks, citing rising rates of obesity and diabetes. Mexico, for example, saw a measurable drop in soda purchases after its 2014 tax. Critics argue these taxes are regressive — hitting lower-income households hardest — and that lifestyle choices shouldn't be punished by government. Should governments tax sugary drinks to reduce health problems, or is this an overreach into personal choice?

students.name: Eric
students.text: I support sugary drink taxes. The data from Mexico and Berkeley actually backs this up — consumption drops, and the revenue often funds health programs in the same neighborhoods. My grandmother developed diabetes in her sixties and the medical costs nearly bankrupted my family. If a small tax nudges people away from drinks that have zero nutritional value, that seems like good policy. We tax cigarettes for similar reasons and nobody calls that an overreach anymore.

students.name: Zoe
students.text: To be fair, I see the public health argument, but I think these taxes end up being unfair. A tax that's the same dollar amount per soda affects a low-income family far more than a wealthy one buying the exact same drink. And it's not like rich households suddenly stop drinking soda — they just absorb the cost. If we really care about health, we should invest in nutrition education and affordable fresh food, not punish people for buying the cheapest beverage available.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: For this week's discussion, let's think about vertical farming as a solution to food security. Vertical farms grow crops in stacked indoor layers using LED lighting and hydroponic systems, with companies like Plenty and AeroFarms operating large facilities in urban areas. Supporters say vertical farming uses dramatically less water and land, and can place fresh produce next to consumers. Skeptics point to high energy demands and limited crop variety. Do you think vertical farming is a meaningful answer to feeding growing cities, or is it a niche technology getting too much attention?

students.name: Marco
students.text: Vertical farming is genuinely exciting. The water savings alone — some operations report using 95% less water than traditional agriculture — are huge in regions facing drought. Plus, growing food in the city you eat it in cuts transportation emissions and gives you produce that's actually fresh, not picked weeks ago. I visited a vertical farm in Newark last summer and the quality of the greens was honestly better than my grocery store. It feels like part of the future, not a gimmick.

students.name: Lena
students.text: I'm more skeptical about this whole approach. The biggest issue is energy — those LEDs run constantly and right now most of that electricity isn't coming from clean sources. So you're saving water but burning fossil fuels. And vertical farms basically only grow leafy greens and herbs, which are maybe 1% of human calorie intake. They don't grow wheat or potatoes or anything that actually feeds the world. It's an interesting niche but I don't think it scales to real food security.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Universal basic income, or UBI, has moved from a fringe idea to active policy experimentation. Stockton, California, ran a pilot giving low-income residents $500 per month with no strings attached, and Finland tested a national program from 2017 to 2018. Advocates argue UBI provides a floor for human dignity and would help workers adapt as automation displaces jobs in fields like trucking. Critics worry about cost, work disincentives, and inflation. Should governments adopt some version of UBI, or are targeted programs a smarter approach?

students.name: Sarah
students.text: I think UBI is going to be necessary, honestly. The Stockton results showed people didn't quit their jobs — they used the money to find better work, deal with emergencies, and reduce financial stress. As more jobs get automated, especially in trucking and warehouses, we need a baseline that doesn't depend on having a specific employer. Targeted programs sound efficient but they come with so many forms and means-testing requirements that people who really need help fall through the cracks.

students.name: Tom
students.text: I think we have to be careful about UBI. At a meaningful level — say $1,000 a month per adult — it would cost trillions of dollars annually. That money has to come from somewhere, either higher taxes or cutting programs that already help specific groups like the elderly or disabled. I'd rather strengthen the existing safety net: better housing assistance, expanded earned income tax credit, real job training. Those reach the people who need help without subsidizing everyone.
